data.title: Different strokes: Huawei’s founder makes subtle dig at lofty pursuits in AI by the US
data.description:
The United States and China are pursuing artificial intelligence in “different directions”, and the divergence is being reflected in the strategies that the governments and companies are adopting, according to the founder of one of the world’s largest technology investors.
The US is focusing on supercomputing power and large models in the lofty pursuit of artificial general intelligence (AGI) and artificial superintelligence (ASI), while China was adopting a more practical approach, using AI to solve real-world problems in development, said Huawei Technologies’ founder Ren Zhengfei.
“The US is seeking AGI and ASI to get answers to questions like ‘What is human?’ and ‘What is the future of society?’ They are trying to solve the whole problem [at once], but it takes time for [humanity] to know what the problem is,” according to a transcript of Ren’s November 14 speech to the International Collegiate Programming Contest, released on Friday.
Ren’s subtle dig at the US approach to AI reflects the strategy behind Huawei’s own AI investments, as the Shenzhen-based company with one of the world’s largest research and development budgets builds its home-grown alternatives to Nvidia’s much-coveted AI processors. The strategic divergence also explains why China is racking up users and developers with open-source large language models, while US technology giants are spending hundreds of billions of dollars on building ever-larger models.

“China is focusing on how to get things done [with AI] to create value and fix development issues,” the 81-year-old entrepreneur said.
China’s AI pursuit is about “safe cities, improvement in public education and healthcare, automated mining and cement factories...and enabling coal miners to go to work in suits and ties”, said Ren. Huawei has pioneered the application of remotely controlled mining machines and highly automated mines in China, connected by its 5G telecommunications infrastructure.
Applications of AI should be the focus of developing countries like Indonesia, Ren told a student from that country at the prestigious global coding competition for university students.
“The most pressing issue [for Indonesia] is not to compete for global leadership in computing power or large-scale models, but rather to focus on applications,” Ren said. “Indonesia has a large number of ports, and the demand for AI is urgent to automate shipping and port operations.”
AI was important for Huawei and the company would focus on communication technology products, such as radio, optical, core networks and data transmission solutions, Ren said.
“In the future, the sense and control of AI will require sending data across thousands of miles, and it’s necessary to create a high-performance network,” Ren said. “Computing power without a network is like an information silo; isolated AI cannot achieve true intelligence.”
Ren said Huawei would work on solving current problems across industries, which aligned with the pragmatic approach of China’s AI development. “Our company’s research will focus on the next three to five years, especially on the application of AI models, big data and massive computing power in the industry, agriculture and technology sectors,” Ren said.
Industrial AI application have been a key strategy for Huawei as the company looks for new directions after being put on a blacklist by Washington, barring it from accessing advanced American technology.
Meanwhile, Ren, who remains the undisputed leader of the Chinese tech giant even though he is not involved in day-to-day operations, said he admired US technology and called for openness.
“The technological power created by America is not harmful but beneficial to the world, and we should support their development,” Ren said. “China must be more open … and refrain from shutting our doors and isolating ourselves,” Ren said.

data.title: Some Hong Kong fire survivors reluctant to relive horror for judge-led panel
data.description:
Some survivors of Hong Kong’s deadliest fire in decades have voiced concerns over reliving the tragedy and sharing personal information if they take part in an investigation ordered by the city’s leader, with psychologists calling for a balance between justice and compassion.
A number of residents from Tai Po’s Wang Fuk Court, which was engulfed in flames last week, told the Post they wanted a fair, just and open investigation that took the feelings and privacy of the survivors into account.
Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu has ordered a judge-led panel to investigate the causes of the fire and its rapid spread. The blaze claimed at least 159 lives, including a firefighter’s, injured 79 and displaced about 4,000 residents.
It remains unclear, however, whether any survivors will be asked to provide accounts, as little has been revealed about how the inquiry will proceed.
Now staying at a hotel in Tsim Sha Tsui, Kwong Pui-lun, a 71-year-old resident of Wang Tao House, said he hoped the inquiry would go “all-out” to determine the facts.
“It must be fair, just and open … I also expect residents to be actively engaged to help uncover the truth,” he said.
“I have confidence in the investigation, all eyes are on it, and the panel will be led by a judge.”
But Kwong said he understood that some residents might struggle if they were asked to give accounts as the process of recalling the traumatic experience could be emotional and exhausting.

Asked whether public hearings should be held, Kwong said some residents might be concerned about privacy and not want to be identified publicly.
A 40-year-old resident surnamed Chan, who lived in Wang Chi House, said residents were not yet ready.
Chan, who works in the construction industry, said his family was still busy settling into a new environment.
“Yes, we need public hearings, but they should be arranged later,” he said.
Paul Wong Wai-ching, a clinical psychologist and an associate professor at the University of Hong Kong’s department of social work and social administration, said that with proper guidance, survivors could be encouraged to come forward.
“Having a strong support system to be prepared for what they might face during the procedure and investigation would help,” he said.
“With some time, 30 to 90 per cent of victims can show aspects of post-traumatic growth, depending on how they restore their narrative,” he said.
Post-traumatic growth, which has no time frame, differs among survivors and could involve spiritual realisations or changes in the way they view their relationships and their motivation in life, Wong said.
Ephraem Tsui Pui-wang, president of the Hong Kong Psychological Society, said he was present during the inquest held after a fire on Mong Kok’s Fa Yuen Street in 2011, which killed nine people.
“I was there on standby in case [witnesses] needed support, but they were very brave and could recount what happened very clearly and showed no signs of distress,” he said.
“They might have gone through a lot of therapy with adequate support and overcome most of their challenges.”
Tsui said such inquiries might only be held one or two years after a tragedy, and only a small proportion of survivors would still be suffering from post-traumatic stress disorder, as most would have been given appropriate treatment.
“The judge will also pay attention to the emotions of the witness and ask if they need to take a break,” he said.
A 42-year-old resident of Wang Chi House, surnamed Wong, said the effectiveness of the independent investigation depended on the powers of the committee and the transparency of its report.
“If it’s like the Lamma Island ferry collision incident or if there are many blacked words in its report, then that does not really help much in terms of how to prevent the whole thing from happening again in the future,” Wong said.
A commission of inquiry was set up to probe the ferry collision in 2012. Although the investigation took only six months, part of the report was redacted to avoid influencing the trials of two coxswains charged with manslaughter. The full report was only released in September 2015, three years after the incident that killed 39 of the 124 on board.
According to transcripts from one hearing, witnesses had to recount in elaborate detail how the tragedy unfolded and were shown photographs and played audio recordings, which caused some to break down in tears.

The judge-led review panel ordered by the Lee has no right to summon or issue a warrant of arrest to those who decline to be present for a hearing, but the city leader has pledged executive assistance to make the process more efficient.
It remains unclear how the panel will investigate the fire, and who will be asked to help, but Lee has also vowed to release the report in full.
Tsui said that in the meantime, affected residents should avoid following the news too closely or repeatedly watching clips of the tragedy to avoid distress.
“We encourage them not to feel guilty for trying to return to a regular lifestyle or finding some joy, but time will be needed to process the incident,” he said.
Wong said he believed some residents would be quite willing to come forward to aid the investigation, despite the emotions involved.
“Most importantly, we must do no harm,” he said. “A balance must be struck between justice, care and compassion.”

data.title: Hong Kong educators call for non-local admissions amid decline in Form One classes
data.description:
Hong Kong secondary schools have 15 fewer Form One classes this academic year, prompting a sector representative to call on the government to allow publicly funded institutions to enrol non-local students in response to the drop in pupil numbers.
The number of Form One classes this school year at 381 government and aided schools taking part in the allocation system has fallen to 1,487 from 1,502 last year, according to the Secondary School Profiles 2025–26 compiled by the Committee on Home-School Cooperation and released on Friday.
Of the 1,487 schools, 16 have to operate one fewer Form One class, while one institution has been allowed to operate one more.
The number of Form One classes in a public school determines its long-term viability. Schools operating only one Form One class must submit a survival plan to officials for approval and will be required to close if their proposals are rejected.
Cheung Chau Government Secondary School failed to admit two Form One classes this academic year and decided to merge with Tang Shiu Kin Victoria Government Secondary School in Wan Chai starting from the next school year.
According to the Secondary School Profiles, a total of four secondary schools have seen their number of Form One classes reduced from three in 2024–25 to two in 2025–26, putting them on the brink of closure.
Two of the four schools are in Sai Kung, one in Tuen Mun, and one in Kwun Tong.
Six other schools have already operated only two Form One classes for at least the past four academic years.
This year, the threshold to operate a Form One class has been raised from 25 students to 27. Some schools may find it even more difficult to secure sufficient enrolment, as the threshold will rise again to 29 students in the next academic year.

Last year, the number of Form One classes in public schools increased by 16 due to the baby boom during the Year of the Dragon in 2012 and an influx of children whose parents arrived from mainland China under talent schemes.
The school sector has already anticipated a continued decline in the number of Form One classes in the coming years, with annual births having fallen to between 30,000 and 40,000 over the past five years.
Lee Yi-ying, chairwoman of the Hong Kong Subsidised Secondary Schools Council, said the slight decline this year was related to the ban on pregnant women from the mainland giving birth in Hong Kong 12 years ago.
In 2013, former Chief Executive Leung Chun-ying introduced a “zero-quota” policy banning women from the mainland from giving birth in Hong Kong. The annual number of births subsequently dropped from around 90,000 to 60,000.
“Due to the influx of children of talent, the student enrolment in Form One did not drop drastically [this year],” she said.
About 49,800 students are expected to join the Secondary School Places Allocation System in 2026, 500 more than last year’s estimate, according to the Education Bureau.
However, given the declining birth rate, Lee called on the government to consider allowing not only direct subsidy scheme schools but also public ones to admit non-local pupils with student visas.
“The number of talent children coming to public schools to study is not stable after all. By promoting Hong Kong as an education hub, the government should consider letting our subsidised schools admit non-local students,” she said.
According to the government, the projected population of school-age Form One students aged 12 living in Hong Kong is expected to fall from 58,800 in 2025 to 49,800 in 2031 – a drop of 15 per cent.
Current Primary Six students who wish to apply for Form One discretionary places must submit their applications to secondary schools between January 2 and 16. Results will be released on March 31. Students without offers will then enter the central allocation system, with results announced on July 7.

data.title: Singapore organisers in limbo over work visa ban on foreign performers
data.description:
For about two years, six nights a week, performers from mainland China, Taiwan and Malaysia have entertained hundreds of guests at the HaveFun Live Show in Singapore, but its organisers are now in a fix ahead of coming work permit changes.
On Monday, the manpower ministry announced that a scheme allowing foreign performers a work permit of up to six months at public entertainment outlets in Singapore would cease in June after enforcement operations uncovered syndicates abusing the scheme.
A spate of operations in September and October exposed individuals who allegedly operated shell public entertainment outlets and hired work permit holders as performing artistes when these companies were non-operational. Some of these foreigners were suspected to have been released to work as freelance hostesses in other public entertainment outlets.
Flint Lu, chief executive of Goodwill Entertainment which runs HaveFun, told This Week in Asia that the news came as a shock to legitimate operators like him.
“I can fully understand where the authorities are coming from but the concern is, will that really resolve the misuse issue or only damage the legal operators? All the legal operators need a solution for this,” said Lu, arguing that syndicates would just abuse other work permits and visas.
“But for us legal operators, the effect is deep and the damage is very heavy,” he added.

On Monday, the Ministry of Manpower (MOM) said in a press release that from June 1, it would stop accepting new applications to hire foreign workers under the scheme, which had been running since 2008.
“In view of the widespread abuse of the scheme, MOM, in consultation with relevant agencies, has assessed that the scheme is no longer serving its original purpose. MOM will thus cease the scheme,” it said.
However, public entertainment outlets could retain their existing foreign performing artistes under the scheme until their passes expired or were cancelled, the ministry added.
The Singapore Nightlife Business Association said in a statement on Thursday that it was asked to provide input on the transition timeline and help ensure that operators had enough time to adjust to the change as smoothly as possible.
“We recognise that this change will significantly affect businesses that have been operating lawfully under the current framework and have expressed to MOM our concerns regarding the cessation of the scheme and its implications for affected businesses,” the association said.
It noted that the six-month pass duration meant firms could retain their workers until their passes expired, with the last group staying on until the end of November 2026.


Jonathan Cham, partner at Rajah & Tann Singapore who covers employment and corporate commercial issues, said the change would certainly pose difficulties for some operators, especially nightlife venues that relied on short-term engagements with foreign artistes.
“It is generally more difficult and costly to transition foreign artistes onto other regular work passes, and not all may be eligible,” Cham said.
“The long lead time to June 2026 and limited short-term exemptions for certain public or government-supported events may help with the transition, but bars and clubs cannot rely on those exemptions.”
He noted that engaging entertainment services through a service provider would leave businesses with less direct control over the performers, as the provider would be primarily responsible for the performers’ employment, including ensuring they had a valid right to work in Singapore.
Businesses that directly hire eligible foreign performers on a regular work pass are likely to incur higher costs, as these permits typically require higher qualifying salaries, according to Cham.
Lu from HaveFun noted that the company had hit the maximum number of foreign work passes it could apply for, given the strict ratios of foreign to local hires.
While the live show does rely on local talent, the company has about 12 foreign performers on performance artistes permits and has regularly relied on them to bring in high quality shows.
He urged for more clarification on the agencies he could use to bring in foreign performers.
“We don’t want to see that Singapore live performances can only engage Singaporeans,” Lu said. “We also need some artistes from overseas, from China, Malaysia, Korea, Japan, from the region, because that will make our nightlife more interesting.”

data.title: Age of AI holds both promise and peril
data.description:
The end of the year is a time for a bit of philosophising, reflecting on history and where we are going in the years ahead.
This year has been dominated by the rise of artificial intelligence (AI), which is becoming the latest field of struggle for leadership between the United States and China. Laymen such as myself dabble in using different models, such as ChatGPT, DeepSeek, Gemini, Perplexity and Qwen. Other than doing basic research and helping to collate larger data sets to extract new insights, how is AI transforming the economy, finance and society as a whole?
I have always been somewhat sceptical of futurologists. It’s not wrong to think about the future because we look to history to try and explain the present, projecting incomplete trends into the future. However, reality is the result of the interaction of so many complex factors that seemingly random events, which Nassim Nicholas Taleb termed “black swans”, can throw our best projections into the rubbish heap of failed theories. There is now a widely shared belief that technology is the solution to all our problems, but every solution brings new problems and challenges.
How is technology changing the global economy? The UN trade body Unctad’s Technology and Innovation Report 2025 explored how inclusive AI can help development. As its secretary general, Rebeca Grynspan, said, “History has shown that while technological progress drives economic growth, it does not on its own ensure equitable income distribution or promote inclusive human development.”
Unctad projects that frontier technologies such as AI, the Internet of Things and semiconductors will grow sixfold from US$2.5 trillion in 2023 to US$16.4 trillion by 2033. Market power, research and development investment, knowledge creation and deployment of these technologies are dominated by technology giants from developed countries. China and the US lead in knowledge generation in frontier technologies, with a growing AI-related or tech divide between developed and developing countries. The more developing countries fall behind, the more they will be marginalised in income and well-being.
McKinsey has projected that generative AI can raise annual US labour productivity by 1.5 percentage points over 10 years and add US$2.6 trillion to US$4.4 trillion in value annually to the global economy. That also implies that emerging markets are able to adopt these technologies quickly so they can catch up.


Since Nvidia alone has a market cap of almost US$4.4 trillion, 10 times larger than Malaysia’s gross domestic product, and major US tech firms are on pace to spend upwards of US$400 billion on AI-related capital expenditure this year, the world is increasingly divided into an elite circle of tech giants at the commanding heights of power and resources versus the rest who are struggling to have enough food, water, energy and social security.
Unctad correctly acknowledges that “the use of AI offers significant opportunities for businesses and countries to grow and to progress towards the achievement of the Sustainable Development Goals. However, it also presents various risks and ethical concerns. Decision makers need to know more about AI if they are to navigate its promises and perils, for sustainable and inclusive development.”
ESG (environmental, social and governance) – the current buzzword for development – aspires to inclusivity, social equality and planetary restoration, but sound governance is needed. Unfortunately, liberalism has not delivered on all of its lofty ideals, and in response some democracies have swung towards the far-right, turning to fundamentalist or populist views to solve their issues.


Whether AI delivers widespread well-being or further slavery is therefore a political question of governance. Can the ageing generation of political and tech leaders understand fully the ethical underpinnings of technology? Will politics control the army or vice versa? Tech, like guns, does not kill people. People kill people using guns and increasingly militarised tech.
So far, the financialisation or monetisation of technology – techfin, as opposed to fintech – has lured many intellectuals into “wanting it now”. Tesla shareholders granting Elon Musk a salary package of US$1 trillion is an example of extreme techfin. We will only understand the full implications when the AI bubble entirely runs its course.
We do not know the complete consequences of our relentless search for progress. The answers will only emerge if we go through another recession like the one in the 1930s, which eventually was resolved through World War II. Every generation must endure pain to know what to avoid in the future.

Those who predict disaster too early are often ignored. Those who remember hard times and how war nearly destroyed humanity are declining in influence. Millennials, particularly those in the advanced economies that have seen only prosperity and peace, have taken unlimited opportunities for granted. They have yet to witness the mass unemployment of the 1930s or the millions of casualties of the world wars.
The financial engineers of today have not lived through the great crash of 1929. Even those who remember the Asian Financial Crisis of 1997 are now a fading minority. Those who went through the global financial crisis of 2007-08 are no longer in the corridors of power. The older generation which went through cycles of boom and bust knows that something big is coming, except we do not know what and when.
What will remain after the latest tech revolution? Our hope is the deeply embedded values of human decency. We live and let live, and life will find a way.

data.title: New Trump strategy shifts US focus from China to Latin America, targets mass migration
data.description:
US President Donald Trump’s administration said in a long-awaited new strategy document on Friday, that the United States will shift from its historic global role towards increasing dominance in Latin America and vigorously fighting migration.
The national security paper, meant to flesh out Trump’s norms-shattering “America First” world view, signals a sharp reorientation from long-standing US calls to refocus on Asia, although it still identifies China as a top competitor.
The strategy also brutally criticised allies in Europe and said that the United States will champion opponents to European Union-led values, including on immigration.
Breaking with decades of attempts to be the sole superpower, the strategy said that the “United States rejects the ill-fated concept of global domination for itself”.
It said that the United States would also prevent other powers from dominating but added: “This does not mean wasting blood and treasure to curtail the influence of all the world’s great and middle powers.”

The strategy called for a “readjustment of our global military presence to address urgent threats in our hemisphere, and away from theatres whose relative import to American national security has declined in recent decades or years.”
The strategy speaks in bold terms of pressing US dominance in Latin America, where the Trump administration has been striking alleged drug traffickers at sea, intervening to bring down leftist leaders including in Venezuela, and loudly seeking to take charge of key resources such as the Panama Canal.
The strategy cast Trump as modernising the two-century-old Monroe Doctrine, in which the then young United States declared Latin America off-limits to rival powers from Europe.
“We will assert and enforce a ‘Trump Corollary’ to the Monroe Doctrine,” it said.
Championing Europe ‘resistance’
Trump has sharply reversed many long-standing US principles since returning to office in January.
He rose to political prominence demanding sweeping curbs on immigration to the United States, fanning fears that the white majority was losing its status, and since taking office has ordered drastic and high-profile raids to deport undocumented people.
“The era of mass migration must end. Border security is the primary element of national security,” the strategy said.

The strategy made clear that the United States under Trump would aggressively pursue similar objectives in Europe, in line with far-right parties that have made strong gains in much of the continent.
In extraordinary language in speaking of close allies, the strategy said: “Cultivating resistance to Europe’s current trajectory within European nations.”
Germany quickly hit back, saying that it does not need “outside advice”.
The strategy pointed to Europe’s lower share of the global economy – which is the result largely of the rise of China and other emerging powers – and said: “This economic decline is eclipsed by the real and more stark prospect of civilisational erasure.
“Should present trends continue, the continent will be unrecognisable in 20 years or less.”
As Trump seeks an end to the Ukraine war that would likely favour Russia gaining territory, the strategy accused Europeans of weakness and said the United States should focus on “ending the perception, and preventing the reality, of Nato as a perpetually expanding alliance”.

Less on Middle East and Africa
The strategy paid comparatively little attention to the Middle East, which has long consumed Washington.
Pointing to US efforts to increase energy supply at home and not in the oil-rich Gulf, the strategy said: “America’s historic reason for focusing on the Middle East will recede.”
The paper said it was a US priority for Israel to be secure, but stopped short of the fulsome language on Israel used even in the first Trump administration.
On China, the strategy repeated calls for a “free and open” Asia-Pacific region but focused more on the nation as an economic competitor.
After much speculation on whether Trump would budge on Taiwan, the self-ruling island claimed by Beijing, the strategy made clear that the United States supports the decades-old status quo, but called on allies Japan and South Korea to contribute more to ensure Taiwan’s defence against potential threats from mainland China.
The strategy predictably puts little focus on Africa, saying the United States should transition away from “liberal ideology” and an “aid-focused relationship” and emphasise goals such as securing critical minerals.

data.title: Taiwan blocks mainland Chinese social media platform RedNote citing ‘high fraud risk’
data.description:
Taiwan has blocked RedNote, a popular mainland Chinese social media platform among young people on the island, citing “fraud prevention” and “information security” concerns.
Taiwan’s Ministry of the Interior’s Criminal Investigation Bureau issued a policy on Thursday announcing an internet shutdown and access restriction order for RedNote, tentatively for one year.
RedNote is a lifestyle social media platform similar to Instagram popular with young people from mainland China and around the world. Taiwan’s interior ministry said RedNote had more than 3 million users on the island – around 1 million more than a year ago.
The block was intended to protect the “information security” of Taiwanese, as the platform had become a “high-risk area for online shopping fraud”, the statement said.


In Beijing, state news agency Xinhua published a post on WeChat on Friday titled “Lai Ching-te’s shameless and boring action! DPP authorities ban RedNote for one year”.
Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including the US, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-governed island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.
Beijing regards Taiwanese leader William Lai Ching-te and his predecessor Tsai Ing-wen, both of the independence-leaning Democratic Progressive Party (DPP), as separatists.
Lai, who took office in May last year, angered Beijing by labelling it a “hostile foreign force” and proposed 17 “national security measures” aimed at countering Beijing’s alleged infiltration efforts.
Taiwan’s Mainland Affairs Council (MAC) stated on Thursday that the block policy was aimed at fraud, not at any particular country’s app, and was unrelated to cross-strait issues.
“Since 2024, the RedNote app has been involved in 1,706 fraud cases, causing financial losses exceeding NT$247.68 million (US$7.91 million),” the announcement said.
RedNote had not responded to requests for comment by the time of publication.
Ma Shih-yuan, deputy minister of the interior and director of the Taiwanese government’s Anti-Fraud Centre, told a press conference on Thursday: “After information security testing by the National Security Bureau, RedNote failed 15 indicators, and there has been no improvement for a long time”.
Ma said these indicators included the app excessively requesting permissions and collecting personal data – such as contacts and biometrics – and that once users agreed to authorise RedNote’s use of their information, it could lead to personal data being leaked or the exploitation of use habits for fraudulent or criminal purposes.
“Further measures will be considered depending on whether the company responds in good faith,” the statement said.
Ma accused RedNote of not cooperating with judicial authorities in accessing data to combat fraud, which he said was unlike companies such as Google and TikTok, leading to “difficult investigations and victims having no recourse for compensation”.
In October, Ma said that Taiwan’s cabinet, through the Straits Exchange Foundation (SEF) – the semi-official Taiwanese body responsible for liaising with mainland China – wrote to RedNote’s parent company demanding improvements but received no response.
Huang Tzu-yi, deputy director of the DPP’s news department, said on Thursday that RedNote was not blocked because of its origin in mainland China but rather because of serious data theft coupled with its failure to respond to the Taiwanese government requests.
Taipei’s announcement also cited mainland regulatory authorities’ past punishment and criticism of RedNote. In September, the Cyberspace Administration of China said it had issued a “severe warning and punishment” to RedNote executives for the presence of “vulgar” and “harmful information” on the platform related to internet influencers. The announcement did not specify the effective date of the order.
Cheng Li-wun, party chairwoman of the Kuomintang (KMT), Taiwan’s main opposition party, posted to Facebook on the issue on Friday.
“RedNote is widely recognised as the least politically charged and most relatable platform. The DPP’s blocking of RedNote under the guise of ‘anti-fraud’ is a restriction on online freedom of speech,” she said.
Jane Guo, a 20-year-old student in Taipei who said she regularly used RedNote for travel information, said she did not feel the platform had the serious fraud problems the island’s government claimed, and that the blockade seemed like a politically motivated action.
Taipei has long been wary of Beijing using social media to spread disinformation, influence public opinion and even interfere in elections.


In 2022, then-National Security Bureau director Chen Ming-tong said that Chinese social media normally put out soft-influence content and could become a platform for cognitive warfare in wartime, but that RedNote and TikTok should not be banned.
Taiwan’s digital affairs ministry on Wednesday said five Chinese apps, including RedNote, posed a cybersecurity risk to users. The ministry said these apps collect sensitive information, access biometric functions and “extract system information”.
In July, Taiwan’s National Security Bureau said the five Chinese apps should not be downloaded or used, and relevant public officials must delete them.
At the time, Beijing’s Taiwan Affairs Office of the State Council accused DPP authorities of abusing their power and “fearing that Taiwanese people would learn about the real mainland through mainland social media and thus become closer to the mainland”.
Additional reporting by Bob Zhao

data.title: As Putin visits New Delhi, can India rely on Russia as a ‘time-tested’ defence partner?
data.description:
President Vladimir Putin’s first visit to New Delhi since the Russian invasion of Ukraine is the clearest sign of India’s determination to balance between bolstering its long-standing defence ties with Moscow and managing trade tensions with the United States, according to analysts.
On Thursday, Putin was welcomed by Prime Minister Narendra Modi at Delhi’s airport for his two-day state visit, before they were driven in the same car to a dinner hosted by Modi at his residence.
Behind the pageantry, India is engaging in complex strategic calculations as it tries to fend off further punitive measures from the US over its trade links with Russia, while Delhi and Washington continue to hold talks on a trade agreement, analysts say.
US President Donald Trump slapped a 50 per cent tariff on India earlier this year, half of which was imposed as a penalty for India’s purchases of discounted Russian oil.
“I think Putin has been very understanding of India’s predicament. They will have to devise ways of trading with subterfuge so that it does not displease the Americans,” said Pushpesh Pant, former dean of the school of international studies at Jawaharlal Nehru University.
Russia has been India’s biggest supplier of military equipment for decades, even as Delhi seeks to diversify its weapon suppliers to include countries such as France, the US and Israel. Up to 60 per cent of India’s military equipment is of Russian origin, including combat aircraft, battle tanks, frigates and an aircraft carrier, according to local media reports.

India is keen to buy more units of Russia’s S-400 Triumf after the missile system played a decisive role in its clash with Pakistan in May, according to media reports. It is also reportedly targeting Russia’s fifth-generation Su-57, either by buying the stealth aircraft or co-developing them with Moscow.
For now, Delhi may choose to buy the lower end of Russian weaponry to avoid incurring the displeasure of the US, India’s largest trading partner, according to analysts.
Delhi could explore buying military components and radars from Moscow instead of fighter aircraft, given that its sectors such as textile and jewellery are reeling from the impact of US tariffs, Pant said.
Pharmaceutical businesses in India could also be vulnerable if Washington were to impose a tariff targeting the sector, he added.
“India has unfortunately painted itself in a corner by aligning so closely” with the US on the trade front, Pant said. “The Americans can pull the plug on India any time.”
Goods trade between the US and India totalled US$128.9 billion in 2024, according to US government data. India’s exports to the US rose by 4.5 per cent to $87.3 billion, with the US posting a $45.8 billion deficit last year.
A key concern for Delhi in its defence ties with Moscow is the long-term reliability of maintenance, supply of spare parts and upgrades for much of its military hardware that still relies on Russian platforms, especially since Russia’s invasion of Ukraine in 2022, according to Uday Chandra, a Delhi-based political analyst.

Both sides would likely work towards defence agreements focused on joint development, manufacturing of spare parts in India and technology transfer, he said.
Such an approach also aligned with India’s self-reliance agenda while strengthening its reputation as a reliable partner for sanctions-hit Russia, Chandra said. “India is not returning to old dependencies. It is stabilising a critical relationship in new geopolitical realities.”
Among the potential areas of collaboration are joint production of components for Sukhoi and MiG aircraft, upgrades of India’s tank fleets and further collaboration in air-defence systems such as the BrahMos missile programme, according to Chandra.
Beyond military hardware, India and Russia could also form new partnerships in nuclear energy and space exploration, analysts say.
Both sides are also seeking to increase two-way trade across more sectors.
On Thursday, Indian Commerce and Industry Minister Piyush Goyal told an India-Russia business forum that the two countries were aiming to increase their trade to US$100 billion by 2030 from almost US$70 billion in the 2025 financial year.
Indian exporters could tap Russia's demand for automobiles, electronics, smartphones, textiles and food products, he said.


However, it is the defence relationship that is likely to remain key to their bilateral ties as the global geopolitical situation continues to evolve, according to analysts.
“Russia certainly would not like to lose a market like India at a time when India is also diversifying its partnerships. And India would want to keep a time-tested partner like Russia engaged in its defence policy because it has yielded dividends,” said Harsh Pant, professor of international relations at King’s College in London.
“Russia’s outreach to India on defence is likely to continue, but how robust that will be needs to be watched out for.”

data.title: BYD founder Wang Chuanfu bets on new tech to win back customers as sales decelerate
data.description:
BYD, China’s electric vehicle (EV) king, is pinning its hopes on next-generation technology to engineer a turnaround in sales amid fierce competition in the world’s largest automotive market, according to its founder and chairman Wang Chuanfu.
Speaking at a shareholders’ meeting in Shenzhen on Friday, the billionaire said the company’s 12,000 engineers – the backbone of the carmaker – would help it regain a technological edge over rivals.
“We have heavyweight new technology in the pipeline,” he was quoted by the official China Securities Journal as saying. “These will be announced later, as it is not convenient for me to disclose more details now.”
He listed faster charging speeds as one of the areas BYD was looking to improve.

Wang also tried to allay concerns among investors about BYD’s competitiveness on the mainland, where it faced a stern challenge from smaller rivals such as Xiaomi and Leapmotor.
In September, BYD, the world’s largest EV assembler in terms of deliveries, lowered its sales target for this year by 16 per cent to 4.6 million units from a previous forecast of 5.5 million, according to Reuters, which cited unidentified sources. The revised target represents a 7 per cent year-on-year increase.
The company’s global deliveries jumped 41 per cent last year from a year earlier.
In November, BYD delivered 480,186 cars, down 5.3 per cent from the same period a year earlier. For the first 11 months of 2025, the carmaker posted an 11.3 per cent increase in sales from a year earlier, with 4.18 million vehicles delivered at home and abroad.
Wang told investors he was confident in BYD’s research and development team and expected technological breakthroughs.
In 2022, BYD dethroned Tesla for the first time as the world’s largest EV builder, buoyed by the popularity of its blade batteries, which were a hit with consumers and other EV assemblers.
The cells of blade batteries are arranged in a more efficient array to increase energy density and enhance resistance to overheating, according to analysts.
“The company’s increasing sales and production volume also enable it to gain an edge over its rivals in terms of costs,” said Chen Jinzhu, CEO of consultancy Shanghai Mingliang Auto Service. “Rivals will be closely watching BYD as it launches products based on its new technologies.”
BYD was betting on exports to increase sales and enhance profitability, Li Yunfei, general manager of branding and public relations, told the Post in September.
Exports were likely to ­constitute about 20 per cent of its total sales this year, up from around 10 per cent last year, he said, adding that the company expected to deliver 800,000 to 1 million cars overseas this year.
BYD also plans to expand its model line-up for overseas markets by introducing its range of China-developed cars to international motorists.

data.title: France poised to get two giant pandas in 2027 after Macron’s visit to China
data.description:
France is poised to get a pair of pandas in 2027 as part of a new decade-long loan agreement finalised following President Emmanuel Macron’s three-day visit to China.
On Thursday, the China Wildlife Conservation Association announced it had agreed with France’s Beauval Zoo to extend their international cooperation on giant panda conservation with the aim of boosting Sino-French exchanges.
According to the group’s statement, the two giant pandas are expected to arrive at the zoo in Saint-Aignan in central France in 2027 for a 10-year cooperation period.
In 2012, the Beauval Zoo welcomed pandas Huan Huan and Yuan Zi under a Sino-French cooperative research agreement.
The two 17-year-old pandas were returned to China last month because of Huan Huan’s chronic kidney disease, a common condition in bears around her age.
On Friday, Brigitte Macron, France’s first lady, visited the Chengdu Research Base of Giant Panda Breeding after a tour of one of the world’s oldest and still functioning irrigation system in the city of Dujiangyan.

She visited Yuan Meng – the first giant panda born in France – to whom she is “godmother”.
Yuan Meng is Huan Huan and Yuan Zi’s baby as well as the first panda cub born in France through artificial insemination and genetic research. His birth in 2017 was hailed as a breakthrough and milestone for European wildlife conservation.
Yuan Meng arrived in China in 2023, as required by the country’s panda diplomacy policy. The French first lady called his birth a symbol of the “absolutely unbreakable friendship” between the two countries.
Since his return to China, Yuan Meng’s condition has been described as very good. Staff at the Chengdu Panda Base told Brigitte Macron during her tour that he had gained 10kg (22 pounds).
Panda diplomacy has been a long-standing practice of China’s in which the bears are sent across the globe as symbols of friendship and soft-power ambassadors.
Chengdu in southwestern Sichuan province is a natural habitat for giant pandas. It houses world-leading panda research facilities in the species’ breeding and conservation.
The French president and first lady’s rare trip to Chengdu was about more than just pandas.
Chengdu and Montpellier in southern France were the first pair of friendship cities established between China and France. Over the past 44 years, Chengdu and Montpellier jointly founded international schools named after each other’s cities.
Chengdu is also a key transport hub between China and Europe as one of the major terminals for the China-Europe Railway Express. Last month, more than 120,000 trains operated on the network, according to state news agency Xinhua.
The French presidential couple’s trip to Chengdu formed part of a three-day state visit to China.
On Thursday, Chinese President Xi Jinping called for deepening bilateral collaborations in the aerospace, nuclear energy, digital economy, biopharmaceutical and artificial intelligence sectors.
Meanwhile, the French president urged “a balanced relationship” between Paris and Beijing and sought China’s help in ending the war between Russia and Ukraine.
The two leaders also signed deals covering energy, agriculture, education and the environment.
